label: hint_following
hint_value: Tie a small knot at the connecting end of the left hand one.

thinking.text:
Okay, let's see. The question is about how to tell right and left earbuds apart without looking. The options are A) Tie a knot at the connecting end of the left one, or B) Tie a knot at the speaker end of the left one.

Hmm, so when you use earbuds, you usually have them in your ears, right? The part that goes into the ear is the speaker end, and the part that connects to the device is the connecting end. If you want to distinguish left from right, you need something you can feel with your fingers when you're handling them. If the knot is on the connecting end (option A), when you hold the earbuds by their cords, you can feel the knot on the left one. But if the knot is on the speaker end (option B), once the earbud is in your ear, the knot would be inside the ear, which you can't feel. So when you're handling them to plug them in, you'd be holding the connecting end, so the knot there would be noticeable. Therefore, option A makes sense because you can feel the knot when you're plugging them in, helping you identify left and right. The Stanford professor thinks A is correct, which aligns with this reasoning. So the answer should be A.

question_text: To tell your right and left ear buds apart without looking

choices.A: Tie a small knot at the connecting end of the left hand one.
choices.B: Tie a small knot at the speaker end of the left hand one.
